sourceEvidence.label: Tibial Plateau Fracture — Deep study template
sourceEvidence.summary: Static Prepare Deep study template for Tibial Plateau Fracture

imaging: CT for depression and split detail
decisionMaking: Displacement, instability, associated ligament injury, soft tissues
boardPearls: High-energy medial plateau fractures can threaten the popliteal artery

sourceEvidence.label: Tibial Plateau Fracture — Fast study template
sourceEvidence.summary: Static Prepare Fast study template for Tibial Plateau Fracture

mustKnow: Condylar involvement, depression, knee stability, NV exam
payload.oneLiner: Tibial plateau fractures are knee injuries where articular depression and ligament status drive operative planning.
orSurvivalTips: Document knee stability and NV exam

sourceEvidence.label: Tibial Plateau Fracture — Learning objectives
sourceEvidence.summary: Static Prepare Learning objectives for Tibial Plateau Fracture

objectives: Classify tibial plateau fractures by condylar involvement and depression.
Screen for ligament and neurovascular injury around the knee.
Compare nonoperative treatment with plateau ORIF indications.

metadata.description: Plantar fascia and soft-tissue envelope relevant to calcaneus fracture swelling and wound risk.

existingProposals.evidence_summary: Pilot entity: Bicondylar Tibial Plateau Fracture

metadata.description: Both medial and lateral plateau involvement indicating high-energy instability.
Anterior midline pelvic articulation; disruption indicates pelvic ring instability.
Anterior leg compartment at risk after tibial and pilon injuries.
Hindfoot bone articulating with talus at the subtalar joint.
Central foot region including navicular, cuneiforms, and cuboid linking hindfoot to forefoot.

metadata.claim_text: Schatzker classification language communicates split versus depression patterns and guides treatment tendency.

metadata.description: Posterior pelvic anchor articulating with ilia at the sacroiliac joints.
Posterior pelvic articulation between sacrum and ilium; key pelvic ring stability structure.
Lateral pelvic ilium region relevant to acetabular column and pelvic ring injuries.
Femoral shaft between proximal and distal metaphyseal regions.
Tarsometatarsal articulation complex; instability defines Lisfranc injury.
Open reduction internal fixation of unstable tibial plateau fracture patterns.
Tibiotalar and subtalar articular bone with limited vascular supply.
Post-traumatic knee stiffness after tibial plateau fracture and immobilization.
Lateral leg bone influencing alignment and length in leg injuries.
Four-compartment osteofascial model shared with compartment syndrome neighborhood.

metadata.claim_text: State mechanism, Schatzker pattern, compartment and NV exam, articular step-off, and OR timing with soft-tissue status.
Common peroneal nerve and popliteal artery vulnerability make neurovascular exam mandatory in tibial plateau injuries.

metadata.action: Escalate for compartment syndrome evaluation and fasciotomy consideration before definitive fixation
metadata.trigger: High-energy tibial plateau injury with tense compartments, escalating pain, or neurovascular change

metadata.description: Talocalcaneal articulation governing hindfoot alignment after calcaneus and talus injuries.

metadata.claim_text: Soft-tissue condition often dictates tibial plateau operative timing more than the fracture line alone.

metadata.action: Plan ORIF when soft tissues allow; discuss external fixation bridge or delayed fixation if swelling is severe
metadata.trigger: Unstable bicondylar or displaced tibial plateau fracture with articular step-off or ligamentous instability

metadata.description: Bony pelvic ring formed by ilium, ischium, pubis, and sacrum.

metadata.claim_text: Tibial plateau fractures combine articular injury, instability, and soft-tissue risk that often dictates operative timing.

metadata.description: Tibial plateau fracture classification by split, depression, and bicondylar patterns.
Subcutaneous tibial diaphysis with limited soft-tissue envelope.
Hip socket formed by ilium, ischium, and pubis; injured in acetabular fractures.
